moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Van't Kruijs Opening, an offbeat choice that prepares f4 while keeping our position flexible. Rather than the standard e4, we occupy the center more cautiously and prepare a quick kingside attack. This move may seem modest, but it gives us control of d4 and f4 while avoiding the theoretical complexities of 1.e4—a practical choice if you want to play your own game.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1.e3 controls d4 and f4, preparing a flexible kingside attack while avoiding 1.e4 theory.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black stakes a claim in the center immediately, which is the principled response to our quiet opening. By playing e5, Black discourages our f4 advance (for now) and prepares to develop naturally. This is standard territory-claiming that Black should be familiar with.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's e5 discourages f4 and prepares natural development, staking a center claim.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop our knight to its ideal square, controlling the d5 and e4 squares while preparing our kingside assault with f4. The knight on c3 also supports the d5 outpost and connects to our development. This move keeps pressure on Black's center and prepares us to open the position favorably.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nc3 develops to control d5 and e4, supports the d5 outpost, and prepares f4 to open the position.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops naturally, attacking our e4 pawn and preparing to support the center. The knight on f6 also prepares Black's kingside castling and adds defender to critical squares. This is solid development that maintains Black's central presence.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight on f6 attacks e4, supports the center, and prepares kingside castling.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Now we execute our plan with a kingside gambit, challenging Black's e5 pawn and opening lines toward the opponent's king. We're sacrificing a pawn to expose Black's center and create attacking chances along the f-file and long diagonal. The payoff is rapid development and an open position where our piece activity compensates for the material.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Sacrifice a pawn on e5 to open the f-file and long diagonal, trading material for rapid development and attacking chances.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black accepts the gambit pawn, which is the critical test of our position. Accepting the pawn commits Black to defending against our attack while keeping the extra material. This is the logical choice, though it does allow us to build pressure quickly.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black accepts the gambit pawn, committing to defend the extra material against White's attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We complete our development with tempo, attacking the f4 pawn and preparing to recapture or push Black's pieces around. The knight on f3 controls e5 and d4, key central squares, while supporting our attack along the long diagonal via e2 or g5 later. Our pieces are now actively placed for the assault we've been preparing.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight on f3 controls e5 and d4, supporting an attack along the long diagonal via e2 or g5.
